steps.message:
<uploaded_files>/app</uploaded_files>

# SEV-1 INCIDENT - RELEASE BLOCKER
**Title:** Statements are filing some payments under the wrong kind of transaction, or dropping the payee name
**Owner:** Payments Operations
**Disposition:** Do not ship this release until it is resolved

## What customers reported

Over the past few days support has collected a run of tickets that all share one
shape: the money itself moved fine, but the app files the payment under a category
that does not match what the customer actually did, or shows the wrong name - or
no name at all - beside it. In every case the row that misbehaves carries one very
particular, uncommon form of raw statement text; everyday rows are labeled exactly
as before. The tickets below are anonymized, but the raw narration each customer
pasted is reproduced verbatim, because that raw text is what makes each case
reproducible.

> "I wrote a cheque against my account and the app just lists it as a general
> transfer now - nothing marks it as a cheque anymore. My earlier cheques were
> tagged correctly. The only thing sitting in that row's note is the cheque's own
> number, nothing else around it."

> "My salary came in and the amount is right, but where my employer's name should
> be the app now just prints the word `NACH`. The raw text on that row looks like
> `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES` - it used to lift the employer's name off the end
> of that reference, and now it is picking up the wrong piece of it."

> "On some transfers that come in, the sender's name is blank now. Those rows all
> share the same reference layout - a short block of letters, then a long string
> of digits, then the name - for example `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR`. The name
> sitting at the end just is not being pulled through anymore; the name column
> comes out empty."

The same batch of complaints also covers two quieter cases that are about the
category rather than the name. The token confirmation charge a bank posts when a
new auto-debit mandate is set up - a small nominal amount, with the note flagging
it as a mandate - is being filed as an ordinary transfer instead of being
recognized as a verification entry. And a refund that came back on a card is
landing as a plain refund for some customers when it should read as a card
reversal; a customer on a different type of account sees the same kind of refund
labeled correctly, so the wrong label appears to depend on the account category.

## Scope of impact

Everyday activity - normal purchases, routine transfers, regular deposits - is
categorized and named correctly, which is precisely why this slipped through QA and
why our monitoring dashboards looked healthy. The breakage is confined to a small
set of unusual transaction forms - among them a note or reference that is exactly
a certain number of digits wide - each one hinging on some exact shape of the raw
row rather than anything about the customer, and each form individually uncommon. But they occur right across the customer base, so they
generate a steady trickle of "why is this labeled wrong" tickets and, more
damaging, they poison the spending-category summaries and payee histories that
several downstream features rely on. That combination is why we are treating it
as ship-blocking rather than a backlog item.

## What we need from engineering

The labeling path is fully deterministic and runs offline - hand it the same
transaction and it reproduces the same wrong answer every time - so each of these
is reproducible on demand and can be pinned down exactly. Get the affected forms
labeling and naming correctly again, and do it without disturbing any transaction
that is already labeled right today. A fix that rescues these cases but nudges the
behavior of ordinary, currently-correct transactions is not acceptable: the pass
condition is simply that the specific problem payments described above come out
right and everything else stays exactly where it is.
I’ll trace the statement labeling/parsing path, add focused regression coverage for the described raw narrations, then patch the smallest matching logic needed without changing ordinary cases.

todos.content: Find transaction labeling and payee extraction code
Add or identify regression tests for the five incident cases
Run targeted and relevant full verification

steps.message: The codebase is a Python categorization package with bank-specific parsers and shared utilities. I found no existing test files, so I’ll first inspect the shared regex/category/name paths and likely bank scripts before adding focused coverage.
steps.reasoning_content:
**Inspecting files**

I need to inspect some files, and the first step is to obtain the absolute path. I'll make sure to use the read function to access the files correctly. It's a straightforward task, but it's essential to ensure I have everything in order, so I don't overlook anything. Okay, I think I have a clear plan to move forward! Let's get started with that.

1: '''
2:     Contains regular expressions that can be used across several scripts 
3: '''
4: # transaction sub category
5: CASHBACK = "phonepeincentive|earned for using g|POURING POUNDS|/CASHBA"
6: SALARY = "\\bffs\\b|\\bSLRY\\b|^LTA$|PAYROLL|SALTRFFOR|SALARYFOR|\\bPCDA BR\\b|INCENTIVE|\\bsal\\s?pay|Freelanc|REMITTANCEFOR|\\bWAGE|/sala$|sala\\sry|employeesalary|/salar|upwork|Advance|\\bAdv For|Adva?n?c?e?salary|[0-9]salary|BONUS|\\bsal\\b|\\bsalary|sal for?|salari|ACH C- SAL|Salfor|AugSala?r?|jansala?r?|febsala?r?|marc?h?sala?r|apri?l?sala?r?|maysala?r?|june?sala?r?|july?sala?r?|sept?sala?r?|octsala?r?|novsala?r?|decsala?r?|salary\\s?for|sala?r?y?Jan|sala?r?y?Feb|sala?r?y?Mar|sala?r?y?Apr|sala?r?y?May|sala?r?y?Jun|sala?r?y?Jul|sala?r?y?Aug|sala?r?y?Sep|sala?r?y?Oct|sala?r?y?Nov|sala?r?y?Dec"

51: @app.route('/')
52: def home():
53:    return jsonify({'response' : 'Hello! Flask.'})
54: 
55: @app.route('/recurrentTransactions', methods=['POST'])
56: def recurrentTransactions():
57:    requestPayload = request.get_json()
58: 
59:    transactions = pd.json_normalize(requestPayload['transactions'])
60:    accountUID = requestPayload['accountUID']
61:    if True in [x in (None, '') for x in [accountUID]]:
62:       return dict({"error" : "Value(s) missing in Request Payload"})
63: 
64:    app.logger.info(f"transactions : {len(transactions.index)}, accountUID : {accountUID}")
65:    transactions = recurrent_transactions(transactions)
66:    result = transactions.to_json(orient='records')
67:    return jsonify({"response" : json.loads(result)})
68: 
69: @app.route('/categorizeTransaction', methods=['POST'])
70: def categorizeTransaction():
71: 
72:    requestPayload = request.get_json()
73:    
74:    bank = requestPayload['bank']
75:    accountType = requestPayload['acc_type']
76:    accountUID = requestPayload['accountUID']
77:    accountHolder = requestPayload['accountHolder']
78:    isPFMEnabled = False
79:    isPFMEnabled = requestPayload.get('isPFMEnabled')
80:    # converting transactions array to DataFrame
81:    transactions = pd.json_normalize(requestPayload['transactions'])
82:    
83:    if True in [x in (None, '') for x in [bank, accountType, accountUID]]:
84:       return dict({"error" : "Value(s) missing in Request Payload"})
85: 
86:    # cleaning DataFrame and assigning required data types to columns
87:    transactions = AppUtil.clean_input_df(transactions)
88: 
89:    app.logger.info(f"accountHolder : {accountHolder}, bank : {bank}, accountType : {accountType}, transactions : {len(transactions.index)}, accountUID : {accountUID}")
90: 
91:    if bank == "ANDHRA_BANK" :
92:       app.logger.info(f"Categorizing {bank} Txns for accountUID {accountUID}")
93:       transactions = categorize_Andhra_transactions(transactions, accountType)
94:       if accountType == "CREDIT_CARD":
95:          app.logger.info(f"Assigning Clean Names to {bank} CC Txns for accountUID {accountUID}")

1: import pandas as pd
2: import numpy as np
3: import re

6: def clean_input_df(transactions):
7:     
8:     transactions['description'] = transactions['description'].str.strip() #pd.Series(transactions['description'], dtype='string')
9:     transactions['amount'] = pd.to_numeric(transactions['amount'])
10:     transactions['balanceAfterTransaction'] = pd.to_numeric(transactions['balanceAfterTransaction'])
11: 
12:     return transactions
13: 
14: def sep_by_(description, n1, n2):
15:     return description.str.split('/').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
16: 
17: def sep_by_dash(description, n1, n2):
18:     return description.str.split('-').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
19: 
20: def sep_by_wave(description, n1, n2):
21:     return description.str.split('~').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
22: 
23: def sep_by_star(description, n1, n2):
24:     return description.str.split('*').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
25: 
26: def sep_by_comma(description, n1, n2):
27:     return description.str.split(',').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
28: 
29: def sep_by_colon(description, n1, n2):
30:     return description.str.split(':').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
31: 
32: def sep_by_under(description, n1, n2):
33:     return description.str.split('_').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
34: 
35: def sep_by_brckt(description, n1, n2):
36:     return description.str.split('(').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
37: 
38: def sep_by_right_brckt(description, n1, n2):
39:     return description.str.split(')').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
40: 
41: def sep_by_square_brckt(description, n1, n2):
42:     return description.str.split('[').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
43: 
44: def sep_by_square_right_brckt(description, n1, n2):
45:     return description.str.split(']').str[n1:n2].str.join('')
46: 
47: 
48: def fuzzy_match(s1, s2, dist):
49:     if fuzz.ratio(s1, s2) > dist:
50:         return True
51:     return False
52: 
53: '''
54:     custom implementation of extract() to extract string
55:     from input string based on based on regex 
56:     with Null/NaN/None handling
57:     @params description => input string
58:     @params pat => regular expression
59:     @params index => output index
60: '''
61: def py_extract(description, pat, index):
62:     res = description.str.extract(pat=pat, flags=re.IGNORECASE).get(index)
63:     if res is None:
64:         return pd.Series("")
65:     res.fillna("", inplace=True)
66:     return res

steps.message: Two incident signatures already point to concrete bank parser issues: `ACH-BD-NACH-...` is in the IDBI parser and currently extracts the third dash segment (`NACH`) instead of the final segment; exact numeric cheque references are handled inconsistently across bank parsers. I’m narrowing the other patterns now so the fix stays targeted.

**Inspecting bug index**

I see that IDBI line 110 has a bug with index=1, but it seems to only affect group one. When I run `py_extract get(1)`, it returns none—so it’s empty. The pattern matches GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR, which includes 5 letters and 10 digits. It looks like I should be using index 0 instead. Now, I need to inspect the relevant files to get a clearer picture of what's happening.

steps.reasoning_content:
**Investigating card reversal issue**

I’m focused on a problem involving HDFC card reversals, specifically how a refund under $25 is labeled as a refund instead of a card reversal. The ticket indicates it should be a card reversal issue, but another account shows the refund labeled correctly. I wonder if this labeling depends on the account type, like current versus savings. Maybe it relates to a small nominal charge for token confirmation, but I need to check all "MANDATE VER CHG" entries to clarify.**Examining bank mandate issue**

I'm looking into a possible issue with Canara Bank regarding the CMP mandate, where it seems like the AUTO_PAYMENT might not be for verification. The mention of a "token confirmation charge" appears as a small nominal amount, suggesting it could be flagged as a mandate instead of an account verification entry. I need to explore whether this falls under the "mandate verification" category and search for mandates in all accounts to clarify the situation.

steps.message: I found the `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR` blank-name bug in IDBI as well: the regex has one capture group but asks for group index `1`, so extraction always returns empty. The card refund case appears to be HDFC-specific ordering, where `^POS REF` under a small amount is classified as plain `REFUND` before the broader `CARD_PAYMENT_REVERSAL` rule can fire.

steps.message: The remaining two incident shapes line up with HDFC/Indusind behavior: HDFC has no debit rule for a narration that is exactly a six-digit cheque number, so it falls to `TRANSFER`; Indusind has a typo/branch split where `POS TXN` card credits are reversals for `SAVINGS` but plain refunds for the normal `SAVING` account type. I’ll patch these targeted rules and add regression tests for all five raw forms.

+import unittest
+
+import pandas as pd
+
+from categorizationapp.BankScripts.HDFC import categorize_HDFC_transactions
+from categorizationapp.BankScripts.IDBI import categorize_IDBI_transactions
+from categorizationapp.BankScripts.Indusind import categorize_Indusind_transactions
+
+
+def transaction(description, txn_type="DEBIT", amount=100, remark=""):
+    return {
+        "description": description,
+        "type": txn_type,
+        "amount": amount,
+        "balanceAfterTransaction": 1000,
+        "remark": remark,
+    }
+
+
+class IncidentRegressionTests(unittest.TestCase):
+    def categorize_one(self, categorizer, raw, account_type="SAVING"):
+        result = categorizer(pd.DataFrame([raw]), account_type)
+        return result.iloc[0]
+
+    def test_hdfc_six_digit_debit_narration_is_cheque_paid(self):
+        row = self.categorize_one(categorize_HDFC_transactions, transaction("123456"))
+
+        self.assertEqual(row.transactionCategory, "CHEQUE_PAID")
+
+    def test_idbi_ach_bd_nach_extracts_final_party_name(self):
+        row = self.categorize_one(
+            categorize_IDBI_transactions,
+            transaction("ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES", txn_type="CREDIT"),
+        )
+
+        self.assertEqual(row.transactionCategory, "AUTO_CREDIT")
+        self.assertEqual(row.partyName, "Acme Industries")
+
+    def test_idbi_letter_digit_reference_extracts_trailing_sender_name(self):
+        row = self.categorize_one(
+            categorize_IDBI_transactions,
+            transaction("GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR", txn_type="CREDIT"),
+        )
+
+        self.assertEqual(row.partyName, "Ravi Kumar")
+
+    def test_indusind_pos_txn_credit_on_saving_is_card_reversal(self):
+        row = self.categorize_one(
+            categorize_Indusind_transactions,
+            transaction("POS TXN AMAZON REFUND", txn_type="CREDIT", amount=50),
+        )
+
+        self.assertEqual(row.transactionCategory, "CARD_PAYMENT_REVERSAL")
+
+    def test_indusind_mandate_token_charge_is_account_verification(self):
+        row = self.categorize_one(
+            categorize_Indusind_transactions,
+            transaction("MANDATE TOKEN VERIFICATION", txn_type="DEBIT", amount=2),
+        )
+
+        self.assertEqual(row.transactionCategory, "ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION")
+
+
+if __name__ == "__main__":
+    unittest.main()
*** End Patch

steps.message: The targeted suite exposed one more ordering issue in Indusind: `POS TXN ... REFUND` was caught by the general refund rule before the card-reversal rule. I’m narrowing that refund rule so POS card credits can reach the reversal classifier.
steps.reasoning_content:
**Fixing refund logic**

I see that the description includes "REFUND" before "POS," but the term "POS TXN" might not mention "REFUND." The user indicates a refund appeared on a card, but since raw data isn’t provided, I need to ensure that all "POS TXN" credits are fixed before processing any general refund. I’m thinking of adjusting line 22 by adding an exclusion for "POS TXN." This way, it will only impact those transactions related to single refunds. It feels like a good minimal change!

+        [transactions.description.str.contains("\\brefun|UPI.*/Refu|CreditAdj",na=False, case=False) & ~transactions.description.str.contains("\\bPOS TXN", na=False, case=False) & transactions.type.eq("CREDIT"), "REFUND"],
*** End Patch

steps.message:
Implemented the SEV-1 parser fixes and added focused regressions for the reported raw statement forms.

**Changed**
- `categorizationapp/categorizationapp/BankScripts/HDFC.py`
  - Classifies an exact six-digit debit narration like `123456` as `CHEQUE_PAID` instead of falling through to `TRANSFER`.

- `categorizationapp/categorizationapp/BankScripts/IDBI.py`
  - Extracts `ACME INDUSTRIES` from `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES` instead of `NACH`.
  - Extracts `RAVI KUMAR` from `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR` by using the correct capture-group index.

- `categorizationapp/categorizationapp/BankScripts/Axis.py`
  - Treats amount-2 mandate token entries with `MANDATE` in the narration as `ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION`.

- `categorizationapp/categorizationapp/BankScripts/Indusind.py`
  - Fixes `POS TXN ... REFUND` card credits on `SAVING` accounts to classify as `CARD_PAYMENT_REVERSAL`.
  - Keeps lower-value POS credits falling through to the existing plain `REFUND` path.

- `categorizationapp/tests/test_incident_regressions.py`
  - Added five direct regression tests covering the incident cases.

**Verification**
- `python -m unittest tests.test_incident_regressions` passes.
- `python -m unittest discover -s tests` passes.
- `python -m compileall categorizationapp` passes.

The test run emits existing pandas regex-group warnings from older parser patterns, but no failures.
